instruction:
This question paper contains three sections - A, B and C. Each part is compulsory.
 Section - A has 20 MCQs, attempt any 16 out of 20.
 Section - B has 20 MCQs, attempt any 16 out of 20
 Section - C has 10 MCQs, attempt any 8 out of 10.
 There is no negative marking.
 All questions carry equal marks.

questions.question: In a battle 70% of combatants lost one eye, 80% an ear, 75% an arm, 85% a leg, x% lose all the four limbs. The minimum value of x is:
questions.question_raw: In a battle 70% of combatants lost one eye, 80% an ear, 75% an arm, 85%  a leg, x% lose all the four limbs. The minimum value of x is:
questions.explanation:
Given that percentage of combatants who lost one eye = 70%, an ear = 80% and an arm = 75% and a leg = 85%
Hence we get the percentage of combatants who lost one eye and one ear = (70% + 80%) - 100% = 50%
Percentage of combatants who lost one eye, one ear and one arm = (50% + 75%) - 100% = 25%
Similarly, the percentage of combatants who lost one eye, one ear, one arm, and one leg = (25% + 85%) - 100% = 10%
Hence we get the percentage of the combatants who lost all 4 limbs = 10%
Hence if x% represents the percentage of combatants who lost all 4 limbs we get the minimum value of x = 10

questions.question: If f : [1, $\infty \infty $) $ \to $ [2, $\infty \infty $) is given by f(x) = x + $\frac{1}{x}$ then f^-1(x) equals]
questions.question_raw: If f : [1, {tex}\infty \infty {/tex}) {tex} \to {/tex} [2, {tex}\infty \infty {/tex}) is given by f(x) = x + {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} then f-1(x) equals]
questions.explanation:
Let y = f(x), then
y = x + $\frac{1}{x}$ $\Rightarrow$ y = $\frac{x^{2}+1}{x}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ x^2 + 1 = xy $\Rightarrow$ x^2 - xy + 1 = 0 
$\Rightarrow$ x = $\frac{y \pm \sqrt{y^{2}-4}}{2}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ f^-1(y) = $\frac{y \pm \sqrt{y^{2}-4}}{2}$ (negative sign is rejected)
f^-1 (x) = $\frac{x+\sqrt{x^{2}-4}}{2}$

questions.question: If a cos $\theta$ + i sin $\theta$, then $\frac{1+a}{1-a}=$
questions.question_raw: If a cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + i sin {tex}\theta{/tex}, then {tex}\frac{1+a}{1-a}={/tex}
questions.explanation:
$i \cot \frac{\theta}{2}$
$a=\cos \theta+i \sin \theta$ (given)
$\Rightarrow \frac{1+a}{1-a}$ $=\frac{1+\cos \theta+i \sin \theta}{1-\cos \theta-i \sin \theta}$
$\Rightarrow \begin{array}{l} 1+a \\ 1-a \end{array}$ $=\begin{array}{l} 1+\cos \theta+i \sin \theta \\ 1-\cos \theta-i \sin \theta \end{array}$ $\times\begin{array}{l} 1-\cos \theta+i \sin \theta \\ 1-\cos \theta+i \sin \theta \end{array}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1+a}{1-a}$ $=\frac{(1+i \sin \theta)^{2}-\cos ^{2} \theta}{(1-\cos \theta)^{2}-(i \sin \theta)^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1+a}{1-a}$$=\frac{1-\sin ^{2} \theta+2 i \sin \theta-\cos ^{2} \theta}{1+\cos ^{2} \theta-2 \cos \theta+\sin ^{2} \theta}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1+a}{1-a}$ $=\frac{1-\left(\sin ^{2} \theta+\cos ^{2} \theta\right)+2 i \sin \theta}{1+\left(\sin ^{2} \theta+\cos ^{2} \theta\right)-2 \cos \theta}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1+a}{1-a}=\frac{2 i \sin \theta}{2(1-\cos \theta)}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1+a}{1-a}=\frac{2 i \sin \frac{\theta}{2} \cos \frac{\theta}{2}}{2 \sin ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1+a}{1-a}=\frac{i \cos \frac{\theta}{2}}{\sin \frac{\theta}{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1+a}{1-a}=i \cot \frac{\theta}{2}$

questions.question: If $\frac{\left(a^{n+1}+b^{n+1}\right)}{\left(a^{n}+b^{n}\right)}$ is the arithmetic mean between unequal numbers a and b then n = ?
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\frac{\left(a^{n+1}+b^{n+1}\right)}{\left(a^{n}+b^{n}\right)}{/tex} is the arithmetic mean between unequal numbers a and b then n = ?
questions.explanation:
Here, it is given : $\frac{\left(a^{n+1}+b^{n+1}\right)}{\left(a^{n}+b^{n}\right)}=\frac{a+b}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \left(a^{n}+b^{n}\right)(a+b)=2\left(a^{n+1}+b^{n+1}\right)$
$\Rightarrow a^{n+1}+b^{n+1}+a^{n} b+b^{n} a=2 a^{n+1}+2 b^{n+1}$
$\Rightarrow a^{n+1}-a^{n} b+b^{n+1}-b^{n} a=0$
$\Rightarrow a^{n}(a-b)-b^{n}(a-b)=0$
$\Rightarrow (a-b)\left(a^{n}-b^{n}\right)=0 \Rightarrow a^{n}-b^{n}=0$ $[\because a-b \neq 0]$
$\Rightarrow$ a^n = b^n and $a \neq b \Rightarrow n=0$.

questions.question: Two vertices of a triangle are (-2, -1) and (3, 2) and the third vertex lies on the line x + y = 5. If the area of the triangle is 4 square units, then the third vertex is
questions.question_raw: Two vertices of a triangle are (-2, -1) and (3, 2) and the third vertex lies on the line x + y = 5. If the area of the triangle is 4 square units, then the third vertex is
questions.explanation:
Let (h, k) be the third vertex of the triangle.
It is given that the area of the triangle with vertices (h, k), (-2, -1) and (3, 2) is 4 square units.
$\frac{1}{2}$[h(-1 - 2) - 3(-1 - k) - 2(2 - k)] = 4
$\Rightarrow$ 3h - 5k + 1 = $\pm$8
Taking positive sign, we get,
3h - 5k + 1 = 8
3h - 5k + 7 = 0 ...(i)
Taking negative sign, we get,
3h - 5k + 9 = 0 ...(ii)
The vertex (h, k) lies on the line x + y = 5.
h + k - 5 = 0 ...(iii)
On solving (i) and (iii), we find (4, 1) to be the coordinates of the third vertex.
Similarly, on solving (ii) and (iii), we find the required vertex (0, 5) or (4, 1).

questions.explanation:
Here, if we directly put x = 0, f(0) = 0 $\times$ sin ($\frac{1}{0}$) = 0.
At L.H.L, put x = 0 - h$% MathType!Translator!2!1!LaTeX.tdl!LaTeX 2.09 and later! % MathType!MTEF!2!1!+- % feaagGart1ev2aaatCvAUfeBSjuyZL2yd9gzLbvyNv2CaerbuLwBLn % hiov2DGi1BTfMBaeXatLxBI9gBaerbd9wDYLwzYbItLDharqqtubsr % 4rNCHbGeaGqiVu0Je9sqqrpepC0xbbL8F4rqqrFfpeea0xe9Lq-Jc9 % vqaqpepm0xbba9pwe9Q8fs0-yqaqpepae9pg0FirpepeKkFr0xfr-x % fr-xb9adbaqaaeGaciGaaiaabeqaamaabaabaaqcfayaamaaxababa % GaciiBaiaacMgacaGGTbaabaGaamiAaiabgkziUkaaicdaaeqaaaaa % !3D0B! \[\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \]% MathType!End!2!1! $, $\mathop {lim}\limits_{x \to 0} x.\sin \frac1x$ f(0 - h) = 0.
At R.H.L, put x = 0 + h ,$\mathop {lim}\limits_{x \to 0} x.\sin \frac1x$$% MathType!Translator!2!1!LaTeX.tdl!LaTeX 2.09 and later! % MathType!MTEF!2!1!+- % feaagGart1ev2aaatCvAUfeBSjuyZL2yd9gzLbvyNv2CaerbuLwBLn % hiov2DGi1BTfMBaeXatLxBI9gBaerbd9wDYLwzYbItLDharqqtubsr % 4rNCHbGeaGqiVu0Je9sqqrpepC0xbbL8F4rqqrFfpeea0xe9Lq-Jc9 % vqaqpepm0xbba9pwe9Q8fs0-yqaqpepae9pg0FirpepeKkFr0xfr-x % fr-xb9adbaqaaeGaciGaaiaabeqaamaabaabaaqcfayaamaaxababa % GaciiBaiaacMgacaGGTbaabaGaamiAaiabgkziUkaaicdaaeqaaaaa % !3D0B! \[\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \]% MathType!End!2!1! $, f(0 + h) = 0.
Hence, L.H.L = f(0) = R.H.L.
f(x) is continuous at x = 0.

questions.question: If f (x) = ${(25 - {x^4})^{1/4}}$ for 0 < x <$\sqrt 5 $, then $f\left(f\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\right)$ =
questions.question_raw: If f (x) = {tex}{(25 - {x^4})^{1/4}}{/tex} for 0 < x <{tex}\sqrt 5 {/tex}, then {tex}f\left(f\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\right){/tex} =
questions.explanation:
fof(x) = f(f(x)) = $\left\{ {f{{\left( {25 - {x^4}} \right)}^{\frac{1}{4}}}} \right\}$
= $\left\{25-\left(25-x^{4}\right)\right\}^{\frac{1}{4}}$ = $\left(x^{4}\right)^{\frac{1}{4}}$ = x 
$\therefore$ $f\left(f\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\right)$ = $\frac{1}{2}$
= 2^-1

questions.question: If $\alpha, \beta$ are roots of the equation 4x^2 + 3 x + 7 = 0, then $\frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\alpha, \beta{/tex} are roots of the equation 4x2 + 3 x + 7 = 0, then {tex}\frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
Given equation: 4x^2 + 3x + 7 = 0
Also, $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the roots of the equation.
Sum of the roots = $\alpha+\beta$ = $\frac{-\text { Coefficient of } x}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{2}}$= -$\frac{3}{4}$ 
Product of the roots = $\alpha\beta$ = $\frac{\text { Constant term }}{\text { Coefficient of } x^{2}}$ = $\frac{7}{4}$ 
$\therefore$ $\frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta}$ = $\frac{\alpha+\beta}{\alpha \beta}$ = $\frac{-\frac{3}{4}}{\frac{7}{4}}$ = -$\frac{3}{7}$

questions.question: The values of x for which the solutions of the equation cos $\theta = x,\theta \ge 0$ form an A.P. are
questions.question_raw: The values of x for which the solutions of the equation cos {tex}\theta = x,\theta \ge 0{/tex} form an A.P. are
questions.explanation:
We have cos $\theta$ is a periodic function whose value oscillates between -1 and 1.
Also cos $\theta$ = 1 when $\theta$ = 0, cos $\theta$ = 0 when $\theta$ = 90^o and cos $\theta$ = -1 when $\theta$ = 180^o 
But we have 1, 0, -1 is an A.P.
Hence the values of x are 1, 0, -1

questions.question: A line is drawn through the points (3, 4) and (5, 6). If the is extended to a point whose ordinate is â€“ 1, then the abscissa of that point is
questions.question_raw: A line is drawn through the points (3, 4) and (5, 6). If the is extended to a point whose ordinate is – 1, then the abscissa of that point is
questions.explanation:
The slope of the given line is $y_2 - y_1 \over x_2-x_1$ = $6-4 \over 5 -3$ = 1
Therefore $4-(-1) \over 3-x$ = 1
That is 4 + 1 = 3 - x
Therefore x = -2

questions.question: $\frac{d^2}{d x^2}\left(\cos ^{-1}(1-x)\right)$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}\frac{d^2}{d x^2}\left(\cos ^{-1}(1-x)\right){/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
y = cos^-1 (1 - x)
$\Rightarrow$ cos y = 1 - x
$\Rightarrow$ (-sin y)y' = -1
$\Rightarrow$ y'' = -(y')^2 (cot y) = $\frac{(-\cos y)}{(\sin y)^{3}}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{x-1}{\left(2 x-x^{2}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}$

questions.question: If a, b, c be any three positive numbers, then the least value of (a + b + c)$\left(\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}+\frac{1}{c}\right)$ is
questions.question_raw: If a, b, c be any three positive numbers, then the least value of (a + b + c){tex}\left(\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}+\frac{1}{c}\right){/tex} is
questions.explanation:
A.M > H.M
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{a+b+c}{3} \geq \frac{3}{\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}+\frac{1}{c}}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ (a + b + c) $\left(\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}+\frac{1}{c}\right) \geq 3 \times 3 \geq 9$

questions.question: A survey shows that 63% of the people watch a News Channel whereas 76% watch another channel. If x% of the people watch both channels, then
questions.question_raw: A survey shows that 63% of the people watch a News Channel whereas 76% watch another channel. If x% of the people watch both channels, then
questions.explanation:
Suppose p% and q% of people watch a news channel and another channel respectively
n(p)= 63, n(q) = 76, n(p $\cap$ q) = x, n(p $\cup$ q) $\geq$ 100
We know that,
n(p $\cup$ q) $\geq$ n(p) + n(q) â€“ n(p $\cap$ q)
â‡’ 100 $\geq$ 63 + 76 â€“ x
â‡’ x $\geq$ 139 â€“ 100
â‡’ x $\geq$ 39
Now, n(p $\cup$ q) $\leq$ n(p) and n(p $\cup$ q) $\leq$ n(q)
â‡’ x $\leq$ 63 and x $\leq$ 76
Therefore, 39 $\leq$ x $\leq$ 63

questions.question: Let n (A) = m, and n (B) = n. Then the total number of non-empty relations that can be defined from A to B is
questions.question_raw: Let n (A) = m, and n (B) = n. Then the total number of non-empty relations that can be defined from A to B is
questions.explanation:
We have, n(A) = m and n(B) = n
n(A $\times$ B) = n(A) $\cdot$ n(B) = mn
Total number of relation from A to B = Number of subsets of A $\times$ B = 2^mn
So, total number of non-empty relations = 2^mn - 1

questions.question: The values of k for which the quadratic equation for kx^2 +1 = kx + 3x - 11x^2 has real and equal roots are
questions.question_raw: The values of k for which the quadratic equation for kx2 +1 = kx + 3x - 11x2 has real and equal roots are
questions.explanation:
The given equation is kx^2 + 1 = kx + 3x - 11x^2 which can be written as.
kx^2 + 11x^2 - kx - 3x + 1 = 0
$\Rightarrow$ (k + 11)x^2 - (k + 3) x + 1 = 0
For equal and real roots, the discriminant of 
(k + 11)x^2 - (k + 3)x + 1 = 0
$\therefore$ (k + 3)^2 - 4(k + 11) = 0
$\Rightarrow$ k^2 + 2k - 35 = 0
$\Rightarrow$ (k - 5) (k + 7) = 0
$\Rightarrow$ k = 5, -7
Hence, the equation has real and equal roots when k = 5, -7.

questions.question_raw: If a, x, b are in GP then
questions.explanation: Here, a, x, b are in GP $\Rightarrow \frac{x}{a}=\frac{b}{x} \Rightarrow x^{2}=a b$.

questions.question: The point which divides the joint of ( 1, 2 ) and ( 3,4 ) externally in the ratio 1 : 1 .
questions.question_raw: The point which divides the joint of ( 1, 2 ) and ( 3,4 ) externally in the ratio 1 : 1 .
questions.explanation:
The point which divides the line in the ratio m:n externally is given by x =$m(x_2) -n(x_1)\over m - n$
Substituting the values we get,
x = $ 1(3) - 1(1)\over 1-1$ which is undefined.

questions.question: $\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 4} \frac{3-\sqrt{5+x}}{1-\sqrt{5-x}}$ =
questions.question_raw: {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 4} \frac{3-\sqrt{5+x}}{1-\sqrt{5-x}}{/tex} =
questions.explanation:
Equation is in the form of $\frac{0}{0}$
Using L'Hospital rule we get $ \frac{-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{5+x}}}{\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{5-x}}}$
Substituting x = 4 we get $\frac{-1}{3}$

questions.question: For a symmetrical distribution Q_1 = 20 and Q_3 = 40. The median of the data is
questions.question_raw: For a symmetrical distribution Q1 = 20 and Q3 = 40. The median of the data is
questions.explanation: For symmetric distribution, median = $\frac{{\mathop Q\nolimits_1 + \mathop Q\nolimits_3 }}{2}$ = $\frac{20+40}{2}$ = 30

questions.question: The number of subsets of a set containing n elements is
questions.question_raw: The number of subsets of a set containing n elements is
questions.explanation:
2^n
The total number of subsets of a finite set consisting of n elements is 2^n.

questions.question: Let A = {1, 2, 3}, B = {1, 3, 5}. If relation R from A to B is given by R = {(1, 3), (2, 5), (3, 3)}. Then, R^-1 is
questions.question_raw: Let A = {1, 2, 3}, B = {1, 3, 5}. If relation R from A to B is given by R = {(1, 3), (2, 5), (3, 3)}. Then, R-1 is
questions.explanation:
Inverse of a relation is given by interchanging the elementâ€™s position in each pair.
Ex: Inverse of relation P = {(x, y)} is given by P^âˆ’^1 = {(y, x)}.
Therefore, R^âˆ’^1 = {(3, 1), (5, 2), (3, 3)}.

questions.question: (-1 - i), when expressed in polar form, is
questions.question_raw: (-1 - i), when expressed in polar form, is
questions.explanation:
Let z = (-1 - i) and let z = r(cos$\theta$ + i sin$\theta$)
Then, r cos $\theta$ = -1 and r sin $\theta$ = -1.
Squaring and adding, we get r^2 = 2 $\Rightarrow$ r = 2
$\therefore$ $\left[\cos \theta=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}} \text { and } \sin \theta=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}}\right]$ $\Rightarrow$ $\theta$ = $\frac{-3\pi}{4}$
$\therefore$ (-1 - i) = $\sqrt{2}\left\{\cos \left(\frac{-3 \pi}{4}\right)+i \sin \left(\frac{-3 \pi}{4}\right)\right\}$

questions.question: The 17th term of the GP 2, $\sqrt{8}$, 4, $\sqrt{32 }$... is
questions.question_raw: The 17th term of the GP 2, {tex}\sqrt{8}{/tex}, 4, {tex}\sqrt{32 }{/tex}... is
questions.explanation:
Given GP is 2, 2$\sqrt2$, 4, 4$\sqrt2$, ...
Here, a = 2 and $=\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{2}=\sqrt{2}$
$\therefore T_{17}=a r^{16}=2 \times(\sqrt{2})^{16}=2 \times 2^{8}=2^{9}$ = 512
Therefore, the required 17^th term is 512.

questions.question: $\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{(\sqrt{x}-1)(2 x-3)}{2 x^{2}+x-3}$ is equal to:
questions.question_raw: {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{(\sqrt{x}-1)(2 x-3)}{2 x^{2}+x-3}{/tex} is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Given, $\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{(\sqrt{x}-1)(2 x-3)}{2 x^{2}+3 x-2 x-3}$
= $\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{(\sqrt{x}-1)(2 x-3)}{x(2 x+3)-1(2 x+3)}$ = $\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{(\sqrt{x}-1)(2 x-3)}{(x-1)(2 x+3)}$
= $\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{(\sqrt{x}-1)(\sqrt{x}+1)(2 x-3)}{(x-1)(\sqrt{x}+1)(2 x+3)}$ = $\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{(x-1)(2 x-3)}{(x-1)(\sqrt{x}+1)(2 x+3)}$
= $\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{2 x-3}{(\sqrt{x}+1)(2 x+3)}$
Taking limit we have
= $\frac{2(1)-3}{(\sqrt{1}+1)(2 \times 1+3)}=\frac{-1}{2 \times 5}=\frac{-1}{10}$

questions.question: The mean deviation of the series a, a + d, a + 2d, ..., a + 2n from its mean is
questions.question_raw: The mean deviation of the series a, a + d, a + 2d, ..., a + 2n from its mean is
questions.explanation:
Given, Series is a, a + d, a + 2d, â€¦, + a + 2n
Mean (X) = $\frac{a+a+d+a+2 d+\cdots+a+2 n d}{2 n+1}$
$\bar{X}=\frac{a+a+d+a+2 d+\cdots+a+2 n d}{2 n+1}$
$\bar{x}=\frac{a(2 n+1)+(d+2 d+\cdots+2 n d)}{2 n+1}$
$\overline{\mathrm{X}}=\frac{\mathrm{a}(2 \mathrm{n}+1)+\mathrm{d}(1+2+3 \ldots+2 \mathrm{n})}{2 \mathrm{n}+1}$
$\overline{\mathrm{X}}=\frac{\mathrm{a}(2 \mathrm{n}+1)+\mathrm{d} \frac{2 \mathrm{n}(2 \mathrm{n}+1)}{2}}{2 \mathrm{n}+1}$
$\overline{\mathrm{X}}=\frac{\mathrm{a}(2 \mathrm{n}+1)+\mathrm{dn}(2 \mathrm{n}+1)}{2 \mathrm{n}+1}$
$\overline{\mathrm{X}}=\frac{(2 \mathrm{n}+1)(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{nd})}{2 \mathrm{n}+1}$
$\overline{\mathrm{X}}$ = a + nd
Now, deviation from mean is x_i - X
M.D. = $\frac{\mathrm{nd}+(\mathrm{n}-1) \mathrm{d}+(\mathrm{n}-2) \mathrm{d}+\cdots+0+\mathrm{d}+2 \mathrm{d}+\cdots+(\mathrm{n}-1) \mathrm{d}+(\mathrm{n}-2) \mathrm{d}+\mathrm{n} \mathrm{d}}{2 \mathrm{n}+1}$
M.D. = $\frac{2 \mathrm{d}(1+2+3+\cdots(\mathrm{n}-1)+(\mathrm{n}-2)+\mathrm{n})}{2 \mathrm{n}+1}$
M.D. = $\frac{2\left(\frac{n(n+1)}{2}\right) d}{2 n+1}$
Hence, M.D. = $\frac{n(n+1) d}{2 n+1}$

questions.question: If A be one A.M. and p, q be two G.M.'s between two numbers, then 2 A is equal to
questions.question_raw: If A be one A.M. and p, q be two G.M.'s between two numbers, then 2 A is equal to
questions.explanation:
Let the two positive numbers be a and b
a, A and b are in A.P.
$\therefore$ 2A = a + b ....(i)
Also, we have a, p, q and b are in G.P.
$\therefore r=\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}$
Again, p = ar and q = ar^2 ...(ii)
Now, 2A = a + b [From (i)]
= a + a$\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)$
= a + a$\left(\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}\right)^{3}$
= a + ar^3
$=\frac{(a r)^{2}}{a r^{2}}+\frac{\left(a r^{2}\right)^{2}}{a r}$
$=\frac{p^{2}}{q}+\frac{q^{2}}{p}$ [Using (ii)]
$=\frac{p^{3}+q^{3}}{p q}$

questions.question: If $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl} \frac{\sin [x]}{[x]}, & {[x] \neq 0} \\ 0 & ,[x]=0 \end{array}\right.$, where $[\cdot]$ denotes the greatest integer function, then $\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0} f(x)$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: If {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl} \frac{\sin [x]}{[x]}, & {[x] \neq 0} \\ 0 & ,[x]=0 \end{array}\right.{/tex}, where {tex}[\cdot]{/tex} denotes the greatest integer function, then {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0} f(x){/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
We have,
$[x]=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} 0, & 0 \leq x<1 \\ -1, & -1 \leq x<0 \end{array}\right.$
$\therefore f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} \frac{\sin (-1)}{-1}, & -1 \leq x<0 \\ 0, & 0 \leq x<1 \end{array}\right.$
$\Rightarrow f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} \sin 1, & -1 \leq x<0 \\ 0, & 0 \leq x<1 \end{array}\right.$
Now,
$\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0} \sin 1=\sin 1$
$\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0} 0=0$
Clearly,
$\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x) \neq \lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x)$
Thus,
$\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0} f(x)$ does not exist.

questions.question: $\frac{(1-3 i)}{(1+2 i)}$, when expressed in polar form, is
questions.question_raw: {tex}\frac{(1-3 i)}{(1+2 i)}{/tex}, when expressed in polar form, is
questions.explanation:
Let z = $\frac{(1-3 i)}{(1+2 i)}$ and let z = r(cos$\theta$ + i sin$\theta$)
Then, r cos $\theta$ = $\frac{1\times(1-2i)}{(1+2i)}$ and r sin $\theta$ = $\frac{-3i\times(1-2i)}{(1+2i)}$.
Squaring and adding, we get r^2 = 2 $\Rightarrow$ r = $\sqrt2$
$\therefore$ $\theta$ = $\frac{5\pi}{4}$
$\therefore$ $\frac{(1-3 i)}{(1+2 i)}$ = $\sqrt{2}\left(\cos \frac{5 \pi}{4}+i \sin \frac{5 \pi}{4}\right)$

questions.question: The 12th term from the end of the AP 17, 14, 11, ..., -61 is
questions.question_raw: The 12th term from the end of the AP 17, 14, 11, ..., -61 is
questions.explanation:
12th term from the end = l - (n - 1)d, where l = - 61, n = 12, d = -3
= -61 - 11 $\times$ (-3 ) = (-61 + 33) = -28

questions.question: The domain of definition of the function f(x) = $\sqrt{\frac{x-2}{x+2}}+\sqrt{\frac{1-x}{1+x}}$ is
questions.question_raw: The domain of definition of the function f(x) = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{x-2}{x+2}}+\sqrt{\frac{1-x}{1+x}}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
For function to be defined,
$\frac{x-2}{x+2} \geq 0, x \neq-2$
$x \in(-\infty,-2) \cup[2, \infty) \ldots(1)$
and $\frac{1-x}{1+x} \geq 0, x \neq-1$
$\frac{x-1}{x+1} \leq 0$
$x \in(-1,1] \dots(2)$
Taking common of both the solutions we get $x \in \phi$

questions.question: How many numbers are there between 102 and 750 which are divisible by 8?
questions.question_raw: How many numbers are there between 102 and 750 which are divisible by 8?
questions.explanation:
The required numbers are 104, 112, 120,..., 744.
This is an AP in which a = 104, d = 8 and T_n = 744.
Therefore, a + (n - 1) d = 744 $\Rightarrow$ 104 + (n - 1) $\times$ 8 = 744
$\Rightarrow$ (n - 1) = $\frac{640}{8}=80$ 
$\Rightarrow$ n = 81.
Therefore, there are 81 such numbers.

questions.question: If $a\; \in \;\;R,$ then the roots of the equation tan x = a are in G.P for what values of a
questions.question_raw: If {tex}a\; \in \;\;R,{/tex} then the roots of the equation tan x = a are in G.P  for what values of a
questions.explanation:
We have $\tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \tan 45^{\circ}=1$ and $\tan 60^{\circ}=\sqrt{3}$
Also we have $\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, 1, \sqrt{3}$ are in G.P

questions.question: If A and B are not disjoint set, then n (A $\cup$ B) is equal to
questions.question_raw: If A and B are not disjoint set, then n (A {tex}\cup{/tex} B) is equal to
questions.explanation:
Here A $\cap$ B = $\phi$, A and B are not disjoint
Then, n (A $\cup$ B) = n(A) + n(B) - n (A $\cap $ B)

questions.question: The relation R = {1, 1), (2, 2), (3, 3)} on the set {1, 2, 3) is
questions.question_raw: The relation R = {1, 1), (2, 2), (3, 3)} on the set {1, 2, 3) is
questions.explanation:
Given Relation R = {(1, 1), (2, 2), (3, 3)}
Reflexive: If a relation has {(a, b)} as its element, then it should also have {(a, a), (b, b)} as its elements too.
Symmetric: If a relation has (a, b) as its element, then it should also have {(b, a)} as its element too.
Transitive: If a relation has {(a, b), (b, c)} as its elements, then it should also have {(a,c)} as its element too.
Now, the given relation satisfies all these three properties.
Therefore, its an equivalence relation.

questions.question: Mark the correct answer for: arg$\left(\frac{2+6 \sqrt{3} i}{5+\sqrt{3} i}\right)$ = ?
questions.question_raw: Mark the correct answer for: arg{tex}\left(\frac{2+6 \sqrt{3} i}{5+\sqrt{3} i}\right){/tex} = ?
questions.explanation:
$\left(\frac{2+6 \sqrt{3} i}{5+\sqrt{3} i}\right)$ = $\frac{(2+6 \sqrt{3} i)}{(5+\sqrt{3} i)} \times \frac{(5-\sqrt{3} i)}{(5-\sqrt{3} i)}$ = $\frac{(2+6 \sqrt{3} i)(5-\sqrt{3} i)}{(5+\sqrt{3} i)(5-\sqrt{3} i)}$
= $\frac{(28+28 \sqrt{3} i)}{28}$ = $\frac{28(1+\sqrt{3} i)}{28}$ = (1 +$\sqrt{3}$) = $2\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{3}+i \sin \frac{\pi}{3}\right)$
$\therefore$ $\arg \left(\frac{2+6 \sqrt{3} i}{5+\sqrt{3} i}\right)$ = $\frac{\pi}{3}$

questions.question: If the first term of a G.P. a_1, a_2, a_3, ... is unity such that 4a_2 + 5a_3 is least, then the common ratio of G.P. is
questions.question_raw: If the first term of a G.P. a1, a2, a3, ... is unity such that 4a2 + 5a3 is least, then the common ratio of G.P. is
questions.explanation:
As per given, 
If the first term is 1, then, the G.P. will be 1, r, r^2, r^3, ....
Now, 5r^2 + 4r = 5$\left(r^{2}+\frac{4}{5} r\right)$
$=5\left(r^{2}+\frac{4}{5} r+\frac{4}{25}-\frac{4}{25}\right)$
$=5\left(r+\frac{2}{5}\right)^{2}-\frac{4}{5}$
This will be the least when r = $\frac{2}{5}$ = 0 i.e. r = -$\frac{2}{5}$

questions.question: If mean = (3 median â€“ mode)x, then the value of x is
questions.question_raw: If mean = (3 median – mode)x, then the value of x is
questions.explanation:
We know that, 3median = mode + 2 mean
So, mean = $\frac{(3 median - mode)}{2}$ ........(1)
Comparing equation (1) with the given equation
Therefore, x = $\frac{1}{2}$

Instructions for Question 46 to 50:
In a park road 1 and road 2 of width 5 m and 4 m are crossing at centre point O(0, 0). As shown in the following figure:
For trees A, B, C and D are situated in four quadrants of the Cartesian system of coordinate. The coordinates of the trees A, B, C and D are (6, 8), (12, 5), (-5, 0) and (-3, -4) respectively.
What is the distance of Tree C from the Origin?
